topics.topic: Class of 2026 entry-level job market: hiring barely up, AI cutting into the bottom rung

self_relevance.rationale: Directly affects every student about to graduate or job-hunt.

comparison_potential.rationale: Varies by employer size and by whether AI is displacing junior roles at big tech vs. smaller employers.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Informs how students plan their job search and where to focus applications.

share_trigger.rationale: Students job-hunting will want to send this to friends in the same boat.

comment_trigger.rationale: Invites 'how many applications have you sent' comparisons.

format_strength.rationale: Strong stat list / reality-check checklist format.

freshness.rationale: Current hiring season, directly tied to this year's graduating class.

evidence_availability.rationale: NACE official labor-market data plus CNBC and Entrepreneur corroboration.

topics.score_summary: Strongest topic of the day: high self-relevance, strong multi-source data, directly tied to a decision students are making right now.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #1 for combining the highest decision-usefulness and evidence quality with multi-outlet corroboration (NACE + CNBC + Entrepreneur).

sources.source_name: NACE (National Association of Colleges and Employers)
sources.title: Hiring Flat for the College Class of 2026
sources.summary: 5.6% projected hiring growth; 45% of employers rate market 'fair', worst since 2020-21.

sources.title: By the Numbers: What the class of 2026 job market actually looks like, and where AI fits in
sources.summary: Entry-level postings down 6-7% YoY; AI linked to 25% drop in recent-grad hiring at major tech firms.
sources.relevance: Quantifies AI's role in the hiring slowdown.

sources.title: New College Graduates Face One of the Toughest Job Markets in Years
sources.summary: Grads describe 100+ applications without a single interview.

buzz.reason: Widely felt anxiety among graduating seniors right now.

topics.content_angle: Reality check: what the Class of 2026 job hunt actually looks like, in numbers
topics.expected_reaction: Validation/relief from students already struggling, plus practical 'where do I even apply' discussion.
avoid: Don't imply AI alone is to blame; growth is also concentrated at smaller employers.

topics.topic: Students use AI everywhere but don't trust it, and worry it's killing their job prospects

self_relevance.rationale: Nearly every student uses AI tools for coursework.

comparison_potential.rationale: Usage/cheating rates vary by school system and discipline.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Informs how students think about disclosing/limiting AI use academically.

comment_trigger.rationale: Cheating-line debate is highly comment-baiting ('where's the line?').

topics.score_summary: Near-universal AI use paired with high distrust and job-security fear, backed by three large surveys.
topics.ranking_reason: Edges out #1 on raw evidence/source count but ranks #2 because the job-market topic has a more concrete, immediate decision hook.

sources.title: Cal State students widely use AI tools, but mistrust results and fear job impact
sources.summary: 95% of CSU respondents use AI; 84% ChatGPT; 82% worry about job security.

sources.title: Survey finds widespread use, mixed feelings about AI among San Diego college students
sources.summary: About 90% use ChatGPT; 80% express multiple AI-related concerns.

sources.title: The largest study of AI use by undergrads is in
sources.summary: About two-thirds use GenAI; 9%+ admit cheating; access disparities by income.
sources.relevance: Largest-scale study, cheating and access angle.

buzz.reason: AI-in-school is a constant hot topic among students.

velocity.reason: Multiple fresh 2026 surveys released within months of each other.

target_fit.reason: Applies to virtually all students using AI for coursework.

source_diversity.reason: Three distinct institutions/outlets (CSU, San Diego regional, UC).

evidence_quality.reason: Large sample sizes, one directly from a university system.

risk_penalty.reason: Must avoid implying all AI use is cheating, but base topic is not high-risk.

topics.content_angle: Myth vs Reality: 'AI helps me study' vs '82% of us are scared it's killing our job chances'
topics.expected_reaction: Recognition/relatability plus debate over where 'help' ends and 'cheating' begins.
avoid: Don't frame all AI use as cheating.
Don't single out one discipline as the worst offender.

topics.topic: Student loan system overhaul July 1, plus record default numbers

self_relevance.rationale: Affects nearly every federal borrower's repayment plan choice.

comparison_potential.rationale: Impact varies by current repayment plan and loan type.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Students/borrowers must actively choose a new repayment plan very soon.

share_trigger.rationale: Practical enough to forward to anyone with federal loans.

evidence_availability.rationale: Bloomberg, CBS News, and NPR all independently reporting the same policy change.

topics.score_summary: Imminent, high-stakes policy change with strong multi-outlet sourcing.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #3: equally well-sourced as #1/#2 but slightly narrower share/comment-trigger potential since it's more utilitarian than relatable.

sources.title: US Student Loan Defaults Reach 9.2 Million as Collections Resume
sources.summary: Record 9.16M in default; 20% of 43M federal borrowers 1+ year behind.

sources.title: A major student loan overhaul takes effect July 1.
sources.summary: Several federal repayment plans eliminated; Grad PLUS ended; Parent PLUS capped for undergrads.

sources.title: Guide: Student loan options change July 1.
sources.summary: Walks through new, more limited repayment-plan choices after July 1, 2026.

buzz.reason: High stakes but more niche than job-market/AI topics.

target_fit.reason: Applies to the large share of students/grads with federal loans.

evidence_quality.reason: Multiple corroborating reports on the same policy facts.

topics.content_angle: Checklist: what changes for your student loan on July 1
topics.expected_reaction: Concern and practical questions about which repayment plan to pick.
avoid: Don't give specific personalized loan advice; keep to general policy facts.

topics.topic: Canada tightens the door on international students while easing work rules for those already in

self_relevance.rationale: Core issue for the international/Canadian-bound student audience.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Directly affects study-abroad and work-while-studying decisions.

share_trigger.rationale: International applicants will want to send this to peers.

comment_trigger.rationale: Likely to draw comparisons with US visa policy.

freshness.rationale: 2026 cap figures and April 2026 rule change are recent.

evidence_availability.rationale: Official IRCC notice plus two independent news outlets.

topics.score_summary: Well-sourced, high-stakes topic but narrower audience (international/Canada-bound students) than the top three.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #4: strong evidence and decision relevance, but audience size is smaller than the broad US-wide topics above it.

sources.title: Canada moves to expand work authorization for international students and graduates
sources.summary: Co-op work permit no longer required as of April 1, 2026.

sources.title: Canada's international student enrolment down to pandemic levels: StatCan
sources.summary: Enrolment fallen back to pandemic-era levels amid tightened caps.

sources.title: 2026 provincial and territorial allocations under the international student cap

buzz.reason: High relevance within its audience, smaller overall reach than US-wide topics.

velocity.reason: Rolling policy story rather than a single sharp news spike.

target_fit.reason: Very high fit for international/Canadian students, lower for general US audience.

source_diversity.reason: Official government source plus two independent outlets.

evidence_quality.reason: Primary government data anchors the claims.

topics.content_angle: Before/After: what changed for international students in Canada in 2026
topics.expected_reaction: Relief over the work-permit simplification, frustration over the tighter intake cap.
avoid: Don't conflate Canada's cap policy with the separate US visa-screening story.

topics.topic: Rising tuition meets a widening pay gap between majors

self_relevance.rationale: Relevant to anyone choosing or reconsidering a major.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Feeds into major-choice and cost-benefit thinking.

share_trigger.rationale: Useful to send to someone debating a major change.

comment_trigger.rationale: Could draw 'my major isn't about money' pushback.

freshness.rationale: Annual-cadence data, not a single breaking event.

evidence_availability.rationale: Two reputable but estimate-based data sources.

topics.score_summary: Solid comparison content but built on annual/estimate-style data rather than breaking news.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #5: strong format fit but lower freshness and narrower source base than the top four.

sources.title: College Tuition Inflation: Rate Increase Statistics
sources.summary: 2026-27 tuition projected up ~3.25% overall, ~2.3% at 4-year schools.

sources.title: 2026 Highest-Paying College Majors
sources.summary: Chemical/computer/aerospace engineering top median salaries; ~$1.9M lifetime gap between highest/lowest paying majors.
sources.reliability: Education ranking site; figures are estimates.

buzz.reason: Steady-interest topic, not a breaking news spike.

target_fit.reason: Relevant mainly to students still choosing/reconsidering majors.

source_diversity.reason: Only two sources, both data-aggregator sites.

evidence_quality.reason: Figures are estimates/projections, not hard survey data.

topics.content_angle: Comparison: what your major is actually projected to cost vs. pay back
topics.expected_reaction: Curiosity about where one's own major ranks; some defensiveness from lower-earning majors.
avoid: Don't frame any major as 'worthless'; salary estimates are not guarantees.

topics.topic: Loneliness and burnout statistics behind viral 'bed rotting' coping habits

self_relevance.rationale: Loneliness/burnout are widely reported among this age group.

comparison_potential.rationale: Some variation by identity group (e.g., LGBTQ+ students report higher loneliness).

share_trigger.rationale: Relatable 'bed rotting' framing is highly shareable.

format_strength.rationale: Works as a data-check format but requires careful, non-meme tone.

freshness.rationale: Based on research published earlier in 2026, not breaking news.

evidence_availability.rationale: Two sources, one a culture outlet summarizing Gallup-sourced data rather than a primary study.

topics.score_summary: Relatable and shareable, but evidence is secondhand and the topic is sensitive enough to require careful, non-meme handling — held for monitoring rather than immediate production.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #6: below the 28-point clean-recommend threshold; flagged for verification and a more cautious tone given the mental-health sensitivity.

sources.title: 5 Takeaways From Recent Research on Student Loneliness
sources.summary: 60%+ of under-25 students feel lonely sometimes/always; 70%+ among LGBTQ+ students.

sources.title: The Real Reason Gen Z Is Burning Out Before They Even Graduate
sources.summary: Gen Z sleeps least of any generation at the same age; 55% try social-media stress habits, 24% specifically 'bed rot'.
sources.relevance: Ties burnout data to a viral coping behavior.
sources.reliability: Culture outlet citing Gallup-sourced data; treat 'bed rotting' figures as the outlet's own framing.

buzz.reason: Relatable but secondhand sourcing limits confidence.

velocity.reason: Not tied to a single fresh event.

target_fit.reason: Broadly relevant but not universally decision-relevant.

content_potential.reason: Needs careful, non-meme treatment given sensitivity.

risk_penalty.reason: Mental-health adjacent topic requires cautious, non-sensationalized framing.

topics.content_angle: Data check: how common is loneliness/burnout really, and what's the most common coping habit
topics.expected_reaction: Recognition and relief that 'it's not just me', but requires careful non-meme tone.
avoid: Don't frame bed rotting as a recommended coping strategy.
Don't conflate normal tiredness with clinical burnout/depression without nuance.

topics.topic: Side hustles have shifted from a trend to a financial necessity for many students

self_relevance.rationale: Many students already work part-time alongside classes.

comparison_potential.rationale: Single source doesn't break this down by school/region.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Touches budgeting decisions but lacks hard data to act on.

comment_trigger.rationale: Could prompt 'what's your side hustle' discussion.

format_strength.rationale: Works as a relatable-quote format but lacks ranking/data hooks.

freshness.rationale: Published a few months ago, not breaking news.

evidence_availability.rationale: Single campus-news source; needs a national survey or labor-data point to strengthen it.

topics.score_summary: Relatable framing but currently resting on one student-journalism source; hold for monitoring until a stronger data source is found.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #7, lowest among the clean-data topics: single weak source caps confidence and total score below the clean-recommend threshold.

sources.title: For Many Students, Side Hustles Are a Necessity
sources.summary: Student journalism piece arguing side hustles are now a financial necessity, not a lifestyle trend.
sources.relevance: Student-press grounding for the side-hustle storyline.

buzz.reason: Plausible but unconfirmed by a wider data source.

velocity.reason: No fresh news event driving this.

target_fit.reason: Relevant mainly to students already working part-time.

content_potential.reason: Needs a stronger data hook to be a full card-news piece.

topics.content_angle: Reality check: how many of us are actually working a side hustle just to get by
topics.expected_reaction: Recognition from working students; possible requests for a follow-up with harder numbers.
avoid: Don't present the single-source claim as a confirmed national statistic.

topics.topic: Common roommate red flags students report, in checklist form (not a single incident)

self_relevance.rationale: Relevant to any student living with roommates, especially new students assigning roommates.

comparison_potential.rationale: Patterns vary by housing type (dorm vs. off-campus) but not strongly by school.

decision_usefulness.rationale: Can inform what to discuss before move-in (cleanliness, guests, allergies, shared costs).

share_trigger.rationale: Highly relatable to send to a future roommate as a 'let's talk about this first' prompt.

comment_trigger.rationale: Invites 'add your own red flag' comments.

format_strength.rationale: Natural fit for a Red Flag Checklist format.

freshness.rationale: Not tied to a specific recent event; evergreen-leaning topic.

evidence_availability.rationale: Single roundup source; needs a survey or housing-office data point to strengthen it.

topics.score_summary: Relatable evergreen topic added to satisfy non-AI diversity at rank 11+, but currently under-sourced and must stay strictly in generalized-checklist form.
topics.ranking_reason: Ranks #11: added specifically as the non-AI counterpart required by the AI-topic diversity rule (1 AI topic at rank 2). Held to a generalized, non-incident-led checklist format because the underlying source includes an individual health-incident anecdote that must not be used as the lead or be identifiable.

sources.source_name: AOL (roommate horror-story roundup, used only for generalized patterns)
sources.title: Roommate horror story roundup (used structurally, not incident-led)
sources.summary: Roundup of student-submitted roommate conflict stories; used here only to extract generalized red-flag categories (cleanliness, guests without notice, shared-cost disputes, undisclosed allergies/health needs), not to retell any single identifiable incident.
sources.reliability: Single roundup source; anecdote-based, requires generalized treatment only.

buzz.reason: Evergreen relatability but not a fresh news spike.

velocity.reason: No recent news event driving it.

target_fit.reason: Relevant to students living with roommates, less so to others.

content_potential.reason: Strong checklist format potential once generalized.

risk_penalty.reason: Underlying source contains an individual health-incident anecdote; must be generalized and never lead with or identify the specific incident.

topics.content_angle: Red flag checklist: roommate conflict patterns to talk about before move-in
topics.expected_reaction: Recognition and 'add yours' comments; some students sharing their own (generalized) experiences.
avoid: Never retell the specific health-incident anecdote from the source.
Keep strictly in checklist/structural format, not story format.
No meme tone given the health/safety angle.

topics_needing_more_sources: Side hustles as financial necessity (c5) — single campus-news source, needs a national survey or BLS-style data point to back it up.
Loneliness/burnout 'bed rotting' (c7) — two sources but one is a culture-outlet summary of Gallup-sourced data rather than the primary study; would benefit from the primary research report.
Roommate red flags checklist (c8) — single roundup source; needs a survey or housing-office data point, and must stay in generalized checklist form rather than incident-led.
strongest_content_opportunities: Class of 2026 job market reality check (c2)
AI use vs AI distrust paradox (c1)
